era_context: Late Bronze Age international system. Egypt under Amenhotep III is in the later phase of his long reign. The Hittite Empire under Suppiluliuma I continues to expand its power and influence. Mycenaean Greece is in the Late Helladic IIIA period. The Shang Dynasty in China is in its mature phase. This is a period of relative stability in the Mediterranean and Near East, with extensive diplomatic and trade networks.

events.title: Amenhotep III's reign continues; approaching end of reign
events.description: Amenhotep III is in the later phase of his long reign (c. 1390–1352 BCE). His reign has been characterized by unprecedented prosperity, extensive building programs, and diplomatic relations with major powers. As he approaches the end of his reign, he may have begun to share power with his son Akhenaten, though the exact timing of this co-regency is debated by scholars. Egypt remains the dominant Mediterranean power.

sources.name: Amarna Letters (slightly later, but documenting this era)
Historiographic consensus on New Kingdom chronology

events.certainty_note: Amenhotep III's reign is well-attested, but the exact timing of the co-regency with Akhenaten is debated. Dating carries ±10–15 year uncertainty.

events.title: Suppiluliuma I's consolidation of Hittite power in the Near East
events.description: Suppiluliuma I (r. c. 1344–1322 BCE) is in the middle-to-later phase of his reign, having consolidated Hittite control over much of Anatolia, Syria, and northern Mesopotamia. The Hittite Empire is now the dominant power in the Near East, rivaling Egypt. Suppiluliuma I maintains an extensive diplomatic correspondence with Egyptian, Mycenaean, and other rulers, documented in the Amarna Letters and Hittite archives.
events.certainty_note: Suppiluliuma I's reign and consolidation of power are well-documented, but precise year-by-year chronology is uncertain. Dating carries ±10–15 year uncertainty.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Amenhotep III's reign and the timing of the co-regency with Akhenaten are disputed by scholars. Some scholars argue for a longer co-regency (up to 12 years), while others argue for a shorter one (2–3 years) or no co-regency at all. This uncertainty affects the dating of events in this year. Additionally, the 'low chronology' for the New Kingdom would shift all dates by 40–80 years.
historiographic_note: This year is poorly documented in surviving sources. No specific events are recorded for this year; the entries above represent the general context of the era. The historical record for this period is dominated by Egyptian and Hittite sources, with limited information from other regions. The chronology of this period is uncertain, with multiple competing chronological systems (high, middle, low chronology) potentially shifting dates by 40–100 years. The surviving sources are predominantly elite and court-centered, reflecting the interests of palace scribes and priests.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of Amenhotep III's reign and cultural achievements.
Continuation of Suppiluliuma I's consolidation of Hittite power.
